questions.question: What is the purpose of the first-pass interview diagram?
options: To show every component that might ever exist in production.
To communicate the main request flow, state boundaries, and scaling shape quickly enough to earn buy-in.
To eliminate the need for spoken explanation.
To demonstrate artistic polish under pressure.
questions.explanation: The diagram should support fast alignment, not exhaustive specification.

questions.question: Which detail is most likely to be deferred from the initial diagram?
options: The presence of durable state.
The primary ingress path.
Low-level internal subcomponents that do not yet change the trade-off discussion.
The distinction between sync and async flow.
questions.explanation: Early diagrams should omit low-value detail until it becomes relevant.

questions.question: Why use a box budget for the first-pass diagram?
options: It protects explanation speed and forces prioritization of the components that matter most.
It makes the architecture more scalable by default.
It guarantees the interviewer will not ask deep-dive questions.
It removes the need to mention caches or queues.
questions.explanation: The budget creates a useful constraint on communication complexity.

questions.question: Which omission is most dangerous in an initial diagram?
options: Using plain rectangles for every box.
Leaving out the stateful boundaries that determine consistency, durability, or scaling behavior.
Not coloring the data path differently from the control path.
Failing to number every arrow sequentially.
questions.explanation: If state boundaries are hidden, the interviewer cannot evaluate core design trade-offs.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with keeping the opening diagram deliberately sparse?
options: It improves clarity and pacing, but some nuance must be added verbally or deferred to the deep dive.
It increases storage cost while lowering compute cost.
It prevents the use of multi-region designs.
It makes consistency discussions impossible.
questions.explanation: The sparse diagram is faster, but not every nuance belongs on the first pass.

questions.question: Which metric best indicates whether diagramming speed is improving?
options: How many colors are used per architecture sketch.
Time to explain the complete primary request flow with minimal clarification from the interviewer.
The total number of service names written on the board.
The number of times the candidate redraws the load balancer.
questions.explanation: Better diagramming shows up in faster, clearer explanation rather than denser drawings.

questions.question: When should a queue appear on the initial diagram?
options: Only when the learner personally likes async designs.
When the queue materially changes latency, durability, decoupling, or failure behavior for the primary story.
Never, because it distracts from the database.
Only after the interviewer asks for detailed implementation.
questions.explanation: It belongs early if it is a meaningful architectural boundary.

questions.question: If the interviewer interrupts before the diagram is finished, what is the best recovery?
options: Insist on finishing every planned box before answering.
Use the partial diagram to explain the main path and add only the next most important missing boundary.
Erase the board and restart with more detail.
Stop using diagrams for the rest of the interview.
questions.explanation: The diagram is a live communication aid, so partial but coherent is better than complete but delayed.
